title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is concentrated in evaluation and governance rather than new capabilities. The strongest updates push on pre-deployment national-security testing, system-card transparency, and evidence that safety properties can diverge from headline performance—especially in clinical and high-stakes decision settings.

items.title: NIST’s CAISI expands frontier AI national-security testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI
items.why_it_matters: It strengthens pre-deployment evaluation channels for frontier models with direct national-security relevance.

items.title: OpenAI publishes GPT-5.5 Instant system card
items.why_it_matters: System cards remain a key primary source on safeguards, evaluations, and deployment constraints.

items.title: Safety and accuracy follow different scaling laws in clinical large language models
items.why_it_matters: It suggests capability gains cannot be treated as a proxy for safety in clinical deployment.

items.title: EQUITRIAGE audits gender bias in LLM-based emergency department triage
items.why_it_matters: It probes whether clinical decision-support models reproduce or amplify documented gender disparities.

items.title: Redefining AI red teaming in the agentic era
items.why_it_matters: It targets faster, more scalable adversarial testing for increasingly autonomous AI systems.

items.title: Stayin' Aligned Over Time examines longitudinal human-LLM alignment with privacy-preserving behavioral data
items.why_it_matters: It argues alignment should be measured over time, not only from immediate post-interaction preferences.

items.title: Feature-augmented transformers aim for more robust AI-text detection across domains and generators
items.why_it_matters: Robust cross-domain detection matters for provenance, moderation, and fraud controls under distribution shift.

items.title: Reinforcement learning is used to reduce false positives in static memory-safety analysis for Rust
items.why_it_matters: Lower false positives can improve trust in security tooling for safety-critical software stacks.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
